summary: DHH reflects on the thrill of discovering technologies and ideas before they reach mainstream adoption. He draws a line from his early embrace of Mac, Ruby, and Campfire through to his current enthusiasm for Linux on the desktop and web development simplification via #nobuild and Hotwire. He argues that recognizing potential in rough, unpolished ideas requires a certain contrarian disposition and willingness to bet on being early. The post frames this pattern of early adoption as both intellectually rewarding and personally energizing.
key_points: DHH sees Linux as currently underappreciated the same way Mac was in 2001 and Ruby was in 2003
Web development has undergone an epoch-altering simplification through the convergence of better browsers, faster mobile CPUs, #nobuild, and Hotwire
Seeing potential in unpolished ideas requires disagreeableness and even arrogance — a belief that the marketplace of ideas isn't perfectly efficient
The compounding effect of multiple incremental improvements matters more than any single breakthrough
money_quotes: Nothing gets me quite as fired up as discovering the future early and undistributed.
That feeling of realizing that something is simply better, and the only reason it hasn't taken off yet is because the world hasn't realized it.
When I discover a path that seems like a clear shortcut, it doesn't really matter if it's poorly paved at first.
It's incredible how much conceptual complexity we've been able to compress in the last decade.
Following such a sense of wanderlust requires a certain disagreeableness. Even arrogance.
A steadfast belief that it's possible that you might actually have found a better way.
You have to believe that the market place of ideas isn't perfectly efficient or perfectly rational.
A life spent without taking at least a few bets on being early on an idea is one not lived to the fullest.
key_insight: The greatest rewards in technology come from recognizing the potential in rough, unpolished ideas before the mainstream catches on, which requires a willingness to be contrarian and bet on your own judgment.
